findings.description: Off-by-one: range(session.total_chunks - 1) skips the last chunk, so every assembled file is truncated (single-chunk uploads produce an empty file) and the subsequent _cleanup_chunks deletes the never-copied final chunk, permanently losing the data.

Path traversal: client-controlled session.filename is joined into UPLOAD_DIR without sanitization, so a name like '../../etc/cron.d/x.txt' or an absolute path (which makes os.path.join discard UPLOAD_DIR) writes attacker-controlled content outside the upload directory.

upload_id is never validated, so an id containing '../' makes the chunk path escape CHUNK_DIR in receive_chunk/assemble/_cleanup_chunks, enabling arbitrary file write and deletion outside the chunk directory.

No size limit is enforced while receiving: chunks of unbounded size are written to disk and MAX_FILE_MB is only checked by the optional post-hoc file_size_ok after storage, allowing disk-exhaustion DoS.
total_chunks has no upper bound, so a huge value lets a client exhaust memory (received set), inodes, and file handles across millions of chunk files.
Files are stored under the raw client filename with no uniquification, so two uploads with the same name silently overwrite each other, letting one user clobber another user's stored file.

The handler is not thread-safe: concurrent assemble calls can both pass the is_complete check and then one raises KeyError on del self._sessions[upload_id], and receive/abort racing assemble corrupts state.
Assembly writes directly to the final destination instead of a temp file plus atomic rename, so a crash mid-assembly leaves a partial file indistinguishable from a completed upload.
verify_checksum reads the entire stored file (up to 200MB or more) into memory with f.read() instead of streaming the hash in blocks.
On checksum mismatch the corrupt file is left in UPLOAD_DIR (only logged), so failed verification never deletes or quarantines the stored artifact despite the module's receive-verify-store contract.
Sessions live only in an in-memory dict with no TTL or persistence, so abandoned uploads orphan chunk files on disk forever and a process restart strands all existing chunks.
start() silently overwrites an existing session with the same upload_id without cleaning prior chunks, so a restart with a smaller total_chunks leaves stale high-index chunk files on disk.
The digest comparison is case-sensitive and expected_sha256 is not normalized or format-validated, so a legitimate uppercase hex digest from the client always fails.
Digest equality uses a non-constant-time string comparison instead of hmac.compare_digest, a timing side channel of low practical impact since the expected value is client-supplied.
Validation is extension-only with no content/MIME sniffing, so e.g. HTML or script payloads inside allowed .txt/.csv files could enable stored XSS or polyglot attacks if files are later served.
verify_checksum and file_size_ok propagate unhandled OSError (e.g., FileNotFoundError) if the stored path is missing instead of returning False.
Upload and chunk directories are created with default umask permissions, so uploaded user data may be world-readable on shared hosts.
User-controlled values (extension, upload_id, derived paths) are logged without sanitization, allowing log injection via embedded newline characters in filenames or ids.
Storage paths are hardcoded module constants rather than injected configuration, hindering testing and deployment flexibility.
assemble fetches the session and then calls is_complete which refetches it, a redundant double lookup.
